title: LA Schools Say 'Enough!' to Too Much Screen Time
summary: Have you ever been told to put down your tablet and go play outside? Well, imagine if your school said you HAD to use a screen all day — even in gym class! That's what happened to Lila Byock's son in Los Angeles. He got in trouble for not having his school iPad during PE. Lila was upset. She started asking why kids needed so much screen time, years after COVID-19 ended. The school didn't have a good answer. So Lila started a group called Schools Beyond Screens. Thousands of parents joined. They all worried that too much screen time was bad for kids. Last week, something big happened. The school board for the Los Angeles Unified School District — the second-biggest in the country — voted to cut screen time for all grades. For elementary kids, they want to get rid of it completely! This is a huge change for a district that used to love putting tech in classrooms. Other states are doing similar things. Alabama, Tennessee, Utah, and Virginia already passed new laws about screens in schools. More than 10 other states are thinking about it. Some people say screens can help kids learn. But others, like professor T. Philip Nichols, say computers haven't made test scores better. He also worries that apps collect kids' private information. "When you read a textbook, that textbook is not reading you back," he says. LA schools have to make the new rules by June. Kids and parents will find out the details this summer. Utah's governor says they're trying to help kids build healthier habits. "We're not going to get this exactly right on the first try," he said, "but we're certainly moving in the right direction."
why_it_matters: This matters because you might get less screen time in school, which could mean more hands-on activities and outdoor play — good for your health and fun!

background_read: During the COVID-19 pandemic, many schools in the United States gave students laptops and tablets so they could learn from home.
Since then, some parents and teachers have worried that too much screen time can affect kids' sleep and attention.
The Los Angeles Unified School District is the second-largest school district in the United States, serving over 600,000 students.
Article_Structure: WHO: Lila Byock, a parent, started a group called Schools Beyond Screens, and the Los Angeles school board.
WHAT: The school board voted to cut screen time for all grades and eliminate it completely for elementary kids.
WHERE: Los Angeles, California, and also other U.S. states like Alabama, Tennessee, Utah, and Virginia.
WHY: Because many parents and teachers believe too much screen time is bad for kids' learning and privacy.

perspectives.description: They feel schools rely too heavily on screens, harming children's health and social skills, and they want more traditional play and learning.
They argue that educational apps and online tools can make learning more interesting and help prepare kids for the digital world.
He warns that apps collect personal data from children without giving much real educational benefit, making tech in classrooms a privacy risk.
